title: SEGMENT 4 — The fucose discovery — Iida and the 2003 paper

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — The fucose discovery — Iida and the 2003 paper

In the late 1990s and early 2000s, Kyowa Hakko Kogyo — a Japanese fermentation-and-pharmaceuticals company that had historically made amino acids and antibiotics — decided to get serious about antibody therapeutics.
They built a research group around therapeutic monoclonals, hired protein engineers, set up CHO cell lines, started characterizing antibody preparations the way modern biotech does.
And within that group, there was a team focused on Fc engineering — on understanding what controlled the immune-effector functions of therapeutic IgGs.

The team was led by people like Mitsuo Satoh, and the experimental work was done by a number of scientists including Naoko Yamane-Ohnuki, Yutaka Kanda, and the researcher whose name became most associated with the discovery, Mizuho Iida.

They had access to something most academic labs didn't: a panel of CHO cell lines making the same therapeutic antibody under different conditions, with different glycoform distributions, and a robust functional assay — antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity, ADCC — to measure how well those antibody preparations recruited NK cells to kill target cells.
What they noticed, looking across their cell-line panel, was a striking correlation.
Some preparations of their antibody were dramatically more potent in the ADCC assay than others.
Same antibody.
Same target cell.
Same NK cells.
But the ADCC potency, measured as the dose required to kill half the targets, varied by more than an order of magnitude across preparations.
That's not normal batch variability.
Something was different.
When they characterized the glycoforms of the more-potent versus less-potent preparations, the answer jumped out.
The potent preparations were enriched in afucosylated species — antibody molecules lacking the core fucose.
The less-potent preparations were dominated by the conventional fucosylated species.
The correlation was clean enough that they could plot ADCC potency against percent afucosylation and get something that looked like a dose-response curve.
To prove causation, they did the experiment everyone wishes they'd thought to do first.
They took the same antibody — the same Fab, the same Fc protein sequence, the same target — and produced it in two different ways.
One way gave them mostly fucosylated antibody, the usual stuff.
The other way gave them mostly afucosylated antibody, with the core fucose missing.
Then they compared the two preparations head-to-head in ADCC.
The afucosylated preparation killed about fifty times better.
Fifty-fold.
At the same antibody concentration, against the same target cells, with the same NK cells in the assay.
The only molecular difference was the absence of one sugar at the bottom of the Fc glycan.

The paper was published in 2002 and 2003 in a series of papers in the Journal of Biological Chemistry and Clinical Cancer Research, and a key one is the 2003 paper by Shields and colleagues at Genentech and Iida's group at Kyowa Hakko — actually, let me be precise: the seminal Kyowa Hakko paper is the 2002 paper by Shinkawa, Nakamura, Yamane, and Iida in JBC, titled "The absence of fucose but not the presence of galactose or bisecting N-acetylglucosamine of human IgG1 complex-type
oligosaccharides shows the critical role of enhancing antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity.
" That title is a mouthful, but it tells the whole story: it's the fucose, specifically, that matters.
Not galactose.
Not bisecting GlcNAc.
The core fucose, present or absent, is the dominant determinant of ADCC.
What's beautiful about this discovery is how mechanistic it was, even from the start.
Iida's group didn't just say "afucose is better.

" They went on to show that afucosylated IgG binds to one specific Fc receptor — FcγRIIIa, the Fc-gamma receptor IIIa, also known as CD16a — with dramatically higher affinity than the fucosylated version.
And FcγRIIIa is the activating Fc receptor on natural killer cells.
So the story coheres: remove the fucose, the antibody binds CD16 tighter, NK cells engage the antibody-coated target more efficiently, the killing goes up.
And critically, the effect was specific to FcγRIIIa.
The binding of afucosylated IgG to the other Fc receptors — FcγRI, FcγRIIa, FcγRIIb — was barely changed.
So you got a selective enhancement of one effector pathway, NK-mediated killing, without changes to other Fc functions.
This selectivity is part of what makes afucose engineering so therapeutically attractive: you boost ADCC without boosting unwanted things like macrophage phagocytosis at off-target sites.
The 2002–2003 papers were a thunderclap in the antibody engineering community.
Up to that point, ADCC enhancement had been pursued mostly through protein engineering — by mutating amino acids in the Fc region to improve receptor binding.
People like Greg Lazar at Xencor were doing beautiful work introducing point mutations that gave fold-increases in FcγRIIIa binding and ADCC.
But protein engineering came with risks: novel amino-acid combinations might be immunogenic, might destabilize the molecule, might fail in development for reasons that had nothing to do with potency.
Glycoengineering offered a different path.
You weren't changing the amino acid sequence at all.
You were changing how the cell decorated a native human IgG.
The molecule was still a fully human IgG, with the same sequence as the natural protein, just with the natural fucose removed.
From an immunogenicity standpoint, from a stability standpoint, from a regulatory standpoint, this was beautiful.
And the effect size — fifty-fold — was bigger than what most protein-engineering mutations could deliver.
So the question became: how do you actually make afucosylated IgG at industrial scale?
You can't just deglycosylate the antibody chemically — that destroys the Fc structure.
You need to make the antibody in cells that don't add fucose in the first place.
And that turned out to be the engineering problem that defined the next decade.
